hook: You already know how to work with finite sums and familiar functions like $\sin x$ and $e^x$ — but what happens when you add infinitely many numbers together, or try to represent any function as an infinite polynomial? This unit bridges that gap, revealing how infinite processes can produce exact, finite answers and unlock powerful tools for computation. Could an infinite sum of fractions like $1 + \frac{1}{4} + \frac{1}{9} + \frac{1}{16} + \cdots$ actually equal $\frac{\pi^2}{6}$?
concept: The unifying idea is that infinite processes — when they converge — can be tamed, tested, and put to work representing functions and computing values with arbitrary precision. Formally, this unit develops the theory of convergence for sequences and series, culminating in the representation of functions as power series.

topic_roadmap.description: You will learn to determine whether an infinite list of numbers approaches a finite value, building the rigorous foundation of limits that underlies everything in this unit.

topic_roadmap.topic_name: Introduction to Series and the Divergence Test
topic_roadmap.description: You will learn to write infinite sums using sigma notation and apply the Divergence Test to quickly identify series that cannot possibly converge.
Apply the Integral Test to connect the convergence of a series to the convergence of a related improper integral, and master the $p$-series rule as a go-to benchmark.
You will learn to determine convergence or divergence of a series by strategically comparing it to a simpler series whose behavior you already know.
Master the Alternating Series Test for series with changing signs and distinguish between absolute and conditional convergence, two fundamentally different ways a series can converge.
Apply the Ratio and Root Tests to efficiently handle series involving factorials and exponentials, the two workhorses for determining convergence in complex cases.

topic_roadmap.topic_name: Power Series and Radius of Convergence
topic_roadmap.description: You will learn to find the interval of values for which an infinite polynomial $\sum c_n x^n$ converges, unlocking the ability to represent functions as power series.
You will learn to express functions like $e^x$, $\sin x$, and $\ln(1+x)$ as infinite polynomials centered at a point, connecting derivatives to power series coefficients.
Apply Taylor series to evaluate limits, approximate difficult integrals, and solve problems where standard techniques fall short, seeing the full power of series in action.
You will learn to represent periodic functions as infinite sums of sines and cosines, extending the idea of series expansions beyond polynomials into the foundation of signal analysis.
